questions.prompt: The lesson summarises a High as "a Low plus what"?

choices.label: More investigation work by the tech.
choices.rationale: Investigation isn't part of the High shape. The SOC has triaged for both.

choices.label: A different SOC team.
choices.rationale: The SOC team is the same; the analyst's classification differs.

choices.label: Mandatory senior sign-off on every action.
choices.rationale: High doesn't require senior sign-off; Critical breaks the shape, not High.

questions.explanation: The customer call. Same components; different tempo, coordination, and stakes.

questions.prompt: The walkthrough cites three things that made this incident High rather than Low. Which set?
choices: Active outbound to a known-bad domain;

choices.label: Customer's MSP tier; the day of the week; SLA clock starting.
choices.rationale: Severity is a SOC classification of activity/target/blast radius, not commercial-tier driven.

choices.label: Number of remediations needed; SOC analyst's seniority; tenant size.
choices.rationale: None of these are severity drivers.

choices.label: Whether the host was on the corporate network or remote.
choices.rationale: Network location isn't a severity driver.

questions.explanation: PowerShell with encoded commands; the affected host has elevated access to financial systems (blast radius if the activity had time to spread). Known-bad infra + attacker technique + elevated-access blast radius. Any one alone might land it in High.

questions.prompt: Put the High walkthrough's major actions in the order they occurred.
items: Claim and review — header, recommendation, in-scope check.
Isolate — agent picks up policy within 30 seconds.
Call the user / IT manager — plain-language explanation plus verify-with-user question.
Approve the scheduled-task remediation once user verification confirms not legitimate.
Verify — task gone, no re-detection in post-remediation window.
Confirm with user (via IT manager) that machine is back working.
Close in Huntress; close in PSA with the running note.
questions.explanation: Claim/review, isolate, call, remediate, verify, checklist + un-isolate, confirm, close. Eight beats; the High shape end to end.

questions.prompt: A peer says: "On a High with isolation recommended, I usually call the user before clicking Isolate — feels less aggressive." What's the failure?
choices: Calling first gives the attacker the time it takes to find the user.

choices.label: No failure — calling first is the polite version of the workflow.
choices.rationale: Inverts the rule.

choices.label: Calling first is fine if the user is a senior executive.
choices.rationale: The walkthrough's scenario IS the senior-executive case and explicitly applies isolate-first.

choices.label: Calling first is fine if the customer's MSP tier is Platinum.
choices.rationale: Tier doesn't license the inversion.

questions.explanation: The right call shape is isolate-then-call — 30 seconds after, not 30 minutes before. The 30-second-vs-30-minute trade. Politeness doesn't license giving the attacker more time.

questions.prompt: 90 seconds in, you clicked Isolate. The portal confirms isolation active. The user calls frustrated: "What did you just do to my machine? I'm in the middle of a board prep." What do you say?

choices.label: "We had to isolate your workstation, please bear with us."
choices.rationale: "Had to" sounds like no choice; tells the user nothing about scope or timing.

choices: Four-move explanation (trigger, action, limits, next step).

choices.label: "I can't talk right now, I'm in the middle of fixing it."
choices.rationale: Hanging up on the affected user mid-incident.

questions.explanation: Then piggy-back the SOC's verification question on the call. The explanation calms her down; the verification question piggy-backs instead of needing a separate call.

questions.prompt: After un-isolation, the user calls back: "I'm back, but I missed a key part of my board prep. We should talk about whether this isolation thing was really necessary." What's the right response?

choices.label: Apologise and tell her you'll make sure it doesn't happen again.
choices.rationale: You can't promise that. Isolation will happen again; pre-promising otherwise sets up a worse moment later.

choices.label: Tell her the SOC made the call and you just executed it.
choices.rationale: Passes the buck; distances you from the action. The customer-facing tech owns the action.

questions.explanation: The activity was real — encoded PowerShell calling a known-bad domain. Isolation contained it before more damage was done. I'm flagging your board-prep impact to the account manager so they can talk with your team about coordinating around high-priority days. The isolation itself was the right call." Honest about the action, honest about the impact, names a destination (account manager) for the relationship piece.

questions.prompt: Why a "running entry" in the PSA on a High rather than writing the note at the end?
choices: On a High with multiple actions and contacts.

choices.label: Huntress requires real-time PSA updates on High incidents.
choices.rationale: Not a Huntress requirement. The lesson recommends running entries as a tech discipline.

choices.label: End-of-incident notes are forbidden on Highs by most PSA configurations.
choices.rationale: Not the framing.

choices.label: Running entries are billed at a higher rate.
choices.rationale: Not in the lesson and not a billing distinction.

questions.explanation: The running entry pays off: you get times right, contact names right, verifications right, without reconstructing afterwards. Reconstruction after the fact loses times and names. Running entries don't.

questions.prompt: The High walkthrough closes in 28 minutes; the Low closed in 11. How does the lesson frame the difference?
choices: Roughly three times as long, which is normal.

choices.label: Highs should always take exactly the same time as Lows; longer means something's wrong.
choices.rationale: Highs ARE longer because of paired actions and contacts.

choices.label: Highs should take 2+ hours; 28 minutes is suspiciously fast.
choices.rationale: 25–45 minutes is the clean range.

choices.label: 28 vs 11 is a sign the tech skipped a step.
choices.rationale: Inverts. 28 minutes is a model of the High running cleanly.

questions.explanation: The shape is the same; tempo, paired actions, and documentation length scale with severity. Tempo and pairing scale; the six-step spine stays the same.

questions.prompt: The activity on the endpoint looks Critical-shaped (ransomware-like, lateral movement, admin-credential abuse) despite the High tag. What does the lesson say?
choices: Bump and let the senior weigh whether to re-grade.

choices.label: Re-grade the severity to Critical in the portal yourself.
choices.rationale: The tech doesn't re-grade. The senior plus SOC do.

choices.label: Ignore the Critical-shaped signals; the SOC's tag is the final word.
choices.rationale: New signal is the surface for a reply; bumping to senior is the right move.

choices.label: Continue the High workflow as written; the signals are coincidence.
choices.rationale: The lesson explicitly says bump when the activity looks Critical-shaped.

questions.explanation: Don't unilaterally upgrade severity. Bump on Critical-shaped signals; don't re-grade unilaterally.

questions.prompt: What stayed the same between the Low and High walkthroughs?

choices.label: Nothing — Highs are completely different workflows.
choices.rationale: Same shape, different tempo.

choices.label: The customer call was the same length in both walkthroughs.
choices.rationale: The Low had no customer call; the High had multiple.

choices.label: The remediation type — autoruns — was the same in both.
choices.rationale: The Low was autoruns; the High was scheduled task.

questions.explanation: Action(s), verify, close in both systems, document. Tempo and paired actions are what differ. Same six-step spine. Tempo and pairing scale with severity.
